content.text: Hooks vs Class Components: The Core Differences

Choosing between hooks and class components is one of the first decisions in any React codebase. Both models are fully supported today, but they differ in how state, side effects, and logic reuse are expressed. Understanding the tradeoffs at a conceptual level — not just syntax — makes migrations smoother and new code cleaner.

attrs.alt: Hooks vs class components feature comparison matrix

content.text: The most important difference is not syntax — it is the mental model. A class component defines an object that persists across renders. Its methods share

, and you attach state and behavior to that shared instance. A function component is called fresh on every render. State and effects are declared at the call-site, and closures capture values at the moment of each render.
This means a function component correctly captures the
inside an effect that ran on render 3, you see render-3's count — not the latest value. Class methods read
which always reflects the current state, which can lead to the opposite problem: reading values that changed since the render that scheduled the effect.
Class components: one persistent object. Function components: a function called on every render.
Class components keep all state in a single
object. Updates are batched but must be issued via
, which merges the new object with existing state. Accessing state before the update commits requires using the updater function form.

call manages a single value independently. There is no merging — you replace the entire slice. Separate state slices also means separate setters, which eliminates the accidental property-drop bugs that
can introduce when you forget to spread existing state.
In a class component, side effects are split across lifecycle methods by
(guarded by manual comparison), and cleanup in
. Logically related setup and cleanup end up in different methods, far apart in the file.
co-locates setup and cleanup in one call. The cleanup function is returned from the effect, so the two never drift apart. You can also have
effects, each focused on one concern — no more cramming unrelated logic into one
or splitting teardown across different lifecycle methods.
There is one timing difference worth knowing:
fires synchronously after DOM mutations, matching
fires asynchronously — after the browser has painted — which is what you want for most data-fetching and subscriptions.

Sharing stateful logic between class components requires either higher-order components (HOCs) or render props. Both work, but both add artificial component layers. A DevTools tree wrapped three levels deep for a single "with auth" concern is a real ergonomic cost and makes debugging harder.
Custom hooks are plain functions whose names start with

. They can call any built-in hook and return any value. Composing them does not add component layers — the hooks run in the calling component's fiber slot. You can share authentication, data fetching, media queries, and form logic as importable functions rather than as wrapper components.
Both models consume context, but the APIs differ in verbosity. Class components use either
(limited to one context per class) or the
render prop pattern. Hooks collapse both to a single call per context.
prevents re-renders when shallow-equal props and state are detected. For custom logic,
gives full control. The downside is that both apply at the component level — there is no way to memoize a specific computed value inside the class.
wraps a function component for shallow-prop comparison, matching
stabilizes function references passed as props. This is more surgical — you opt in per-expression rather than per-component.
A common mistake is over-memoizing. Wrapping every value in
adds overhead without benefit unless those values flow into a
boundary or a deps array. Measure first.
Error boundaries are the one area where class components remain required. The

lifecycle methods have no hook equivalents in React 18. A minimal error boundary class component can be imported once and used to wrap any subtree, which limits the class component footprint to a single utility file.
Testing class components often involves rendering the component and calling lifecycle methods manually or relying on enzyme's shallow rendering. Because state lives on
, tests sometimes reach into the component instance to read or set state directly — coupling the test to implementation details.
Testing with hooks follows React Testing Library's philosophy: render the component and interact with it as a user would. Custom hooks can be tested in isolation with
. There is no component instance to reach into — the test drives the UI, not the implementation.

React's class and function components coexist freely. A class component can import and render a function component and vice versa. The safest migration strategy is leaf-first: convert small stateless components first, then work up to components with state and effects as confidence grows. Never rewrite a class component that is working correctly — migrate on the occasion of a feature change or refactor.

content: : convert small, stateless components first. Zero risk, builds confidence.
: when tackling stateful components, map each lifecycle method to its hook equivalent before writing code.
: hooks and classes interoperate, so partial conversions are safe stopping points.
: there is nothing to migrate — they stay as-is.

content.text: The most common pitfall when migrating is treating
. It runs at the same point in time but captures the initial render's closure values, unlike
at call-time. If the effect references state or props, include them in the dependency array.
When to Still Write a Class Component

Class components are the correct choice for error boundaries and for legacy codebases with deep class hierarchies where a full rewrite is not justified. They are also necessary when integrating with third-party libraries that depend on lifecycle hooks not yet exposed as hooks. Outside these cases, new code should use function components.
Legacy codebase file, no current task
Working code should not be rewritten unnecessarily
Bridge pattern — wrap in a class, expose to hooks via context
Hooks and class components are equivalent in what they can render, but they differ in how logic is organized and shared. Hooks enable co-location of related concerns, eliminate

binding issues, and make stateful logic composable without wrapper components. Class components remain necessary only for error boundaries. Migrating leaf-first and understanding the closure-vs-instance mental model difference avoids the most common pitfalls.

content: : class = persistent object; function = called fresh per render, closures capture render-time values
: lifecycle methods split by phase vs
: HOCs / render props (wrapper layers) vs custom hooks (plain functions)
: class components only — no hook equivalent
: leaf-first, coexistence is fine, do not rewrite working code without a reason
